q: What is the process of impressing a low-frequency information signal onto a high-frequency carrier signal called?

What is another name for amplitude modulation in terms of frequency translation?
The shape of the amplitude-modulated wave is called the _____.
What is the ratio of the modulating signal peak voltage to the carrier peak voltage called?
When does overmodulation occur in an AM signal?
a: When Vm is less than Vc
b: When Vm is equal to Vc
c: When Vm is greater than Vc

q: What is the result of overmodulation in AM transmitters?
What is the official ITU emission designation for standard amplitude modulation with full carrier and double sidebands?
What is the official ITU emission designation for a single-sideband, suppressed-carrier signal?
What is the official ITU emission designation for independent sideband (ISB) transmission?
What is the official ITU emission designation for vestigial sideband (VSB) transmission used in television?
What is the official ITU emission designation for single-sideband, reduced-carrier transmission?
Which of the following is not a primary advantage of SSB over standard AM?
What component is used to suppress the carrier in an SSB generator?
What type of filter is most commonly used in SSB transmitters to eliminate the unwanted sideband?
In an AM transmitter, what is the purpose of the buffer amplifier stage?
a: To amplify the audio signal to high power levels
b: To isolate the master oscillator from load variations
d: To match the antenna impedance to the transmission line

q: What is the main advantage of the phase-shift method of SSB generation over the filter method?
a: It requires no sharp bandpass filter
b: It is much simpler to align and maintain
d: It provides much higher carrier suppression

q: Which diode is specifically designed to exhibit variable capacitance when reverse biased?
What process refers to transforming a received modulated RF signal back into its original baseband format?
What circuit is typically used as the second detector in a standard AM receiver?
What determines the bandwidth of a transmitted standard AM signal?
a: The maximum amplitude of the modulating signal
c: Twice the highest frequency of the modulating signal
d: The intermediate frequency of the receiver

q: What circuit performs mathematical multiplication on two input signals in the frequency domain?
What is the function of an Automatic Gain Control (AGC) circuit in an AM receiver?
a: To keep the local oscillator frequency constant
b: To maintain a relatively constant audio volume output over a wide range of input RF signal strengths
c: To increase the selectivity of the IF bandpass filter

q: What occurs when the modulation index 'm' of an AM signal is exactly equal to zero?
a: The signal is unmodulated carrier only
b: The sidebands are at their maximum power levels
c: The carrier power is reduced to zero
d: The envelope has severe clipping distortion

q: What is the term for a receiver's ability to accept a desired band of frequencies and reject all others?
What is the term for a receiver's ability to amplify weak RF signals to a usable level?
What is the term for a receiver's ability to faithfully reproduce the original baseband intelligence?
What type of receiver is designed to overcome the tracking and selectivity challenges of tuned radio-frequency (TRF) receivers?
In superheterodyne receivers, what circuit stage performs the frequency translation to the intermediate frequency (IF)?
What is the standard intermediate frequency (IF) used in commercial AM broadcast band receivers?
What is the term for an unwanted signal frequency that is equal to the tuned station frequency plus twice the intermediate frequency?
How can the image frequency rejection of a superheterodyne receiver be significantly improved?
a: By adding an RF preamplifier stage before the mixer
b: By increasing the gain of the audio amplifier
c: By narrowing the bandwidth of the detector circuit
d: By lowering the local oscillator frequency

q: What is the primary function of a limiter circuit in an FM receiver?
a: To remove amplitude variations and noise before demodulation
b: To limit the maximum volume of the audio amplifier
c: To restrict the bandwidth of the IF stage
d: To keep the local oscillator from drifting off frequency

q: What circuit is commonly used to demodulate FM signals by converting frequency variations directly into voltage variations?
In FM, what occurs to the carrier deviation when the amplitude of the modulating signal is decreased?
What type of modulation is produced when the phase angle of a sinusoidal carrier is varied in proportion to the modulating signal?
What parameters of an FM signal remain constant at all times?
a: Total power and amplitude of the modulated wave

q: According to Carson's Rule, what is the approximate bandwidth required for an FM wave?
a: Twice the sum of the peak frequency deviation and the highest modulating frequency
c: Equal to the peak frequency deviation

q: What phenomenon occurs in FM receivers where a stronger signal completely suppresses a weaker co-channel signal?
What circuit stage is aligned first in an FM receiver after the IF stages are completed?
What modulation technique is used to transmit digital data by shifting the frequency of the carrier between two discrete values?
What is the primary advantage of FSK over ASK transmission?
a: Much greater resistance to noise and amplitude variations

q: What is the standard intermediate frequency (IF) for commercial FM receivers?
What type of modulation varies the phase angle of the carrier between discrete values to represent binary data?
Which digital modulation technique represents binary data using 16 combinations of amplitude and phase?
What process converts continuous analog signals into a sequence of digital pulses in PCM?
What is the difference between the original analog sample and its corresponding quantized representation?
What are the three essential steps required to produce a PCM signal?
What is the primary purpose of companding in PCM systems?
a: To protect weaker signals from quantization distortion
b: To compress the RF bandwidth of the transmission line
c: To convert serial streams into parallel data buses
d: To eliminate the need for anti-aliasing filters

q: Which pulse modulation system is fundamentally analog in nature?
What is the principal disadvantage of PCM systems?
d: High error rates introduced by quantizing noise

q: What is the line speed of a standard Bell System T1 carrier line?
How many voice channels are multiplexed together in a standard FDM supergroup?
What device is used to interface digital computer signals to an analog voice-grade telephone line?
What occurs when the modulation index 'm' of an AM signal is greater than 1?
a: The carrier is overmodulated and the envelope is distorted
b: The unmodulated carrier power drops to zero
d: The frequency of the carrier changes linearly

q: What circuit is most commonly used to track and lock onto incoming carrier frequencies in an FM receiver?
What term describes the range of frequencies over which a PLL can maintain synchronization with an incoming signal?
When a PLL is fully locked, what is the frequency difference between the input signal and the VCO?
d: Equal to the loop filter bandwidth

q: What digital modulation technique is characterized by a binary pulse stream that shifts between two discrete voltage levels to represent digital states?
What method of generating an SSB signal uses only one balanced modulator circuit?
What type of modulation is used by the stereophonic subcarrier in commercial FM broadcasting?
What is the frequency of the pilot signal used for stereo FM broadcasting?
In a digital communications system, what is 'baud' a measure of?
a: The signaling rate or number of symbols transmitted per second
b: The total number of binary bits transmitted per second
c: The bandwidth efficiency of the digital modulator
d: The frequency deviation of the carrier

q: What theorem sets the absolute limit on the maximum capacity of a communication channel in the presence of noise?
Which digital modulation technique has the highest bandwidth efficiency among standard formats?
What is the primary cause of slope overload distortion in delta modulation systems?
a: The input analog signal changes faster than the step size can track
b: The sampling rate is too high
c: The step size is too large
d: The input signal amplitude is too small

q: What is the minimum number of bits required to encode 64 unique quantization levels?
Under what condition does an analog FM signal produce only a single pair of significant sidebands?
a: When the modulation index is much less than 1 (narrowband FM)
b: When the modulation index is equal to 1
c: When the modulating frequency is zero
d: When the frequency deviation is equal to the carrier frequency

q: What type of amplifier is typically used to amplify modulated RF signals without introducing distortion?
a: Linear class B or class AB amplifier

q: In an FM system, when the modulating frequency is doubled while the modulating voltage remains constant, what occurs to the modulation index?

Which noise type occurs specifically during the quantization process in a digital-to-analog converter?
What mathematical operation is performed by a balanced modulator circuit?
